back_translation: Now, on one side, that first covenant had regulations for worship, and also a place of worship on the earth.
For there was a tabernacle that was prepared. In its first room there were the lampstand, the table, and the consecrated offering-bread; this room was called the Holy Place.
And behind the second curtain, there was another room of the tabernacle, which was called the Most Holy Place.

In that room there was the gold incense-burning altar, and there was the ark of the covenant overlaid with gold on every side. Inside that ark were a gold jar containing manna, the rod of Aaron that had budded, and the stone tablets of the covenant.
And above that ark were cherubim of glory, who covered the mercy seat with their wings; but concerning these things, now is not the time for us to discuss them in detail.
When these things had been so prepared, on one side, the priests entered the first room of the tabernacle regularly, to perform their duties of service.

But on the other side, only the high priest entered into the second room, once each year, and he did not enter without blood, which he offered for himself, and for the offenses that the people had committed unknowingly.
By this matter, the Holy Spirit was disclosing that the way of access into the Most Holy Place had not yet been disclosed, as long as that first tabernacle was still standing.
This thing is a parable-symbol for the present time, indicating that the offerings and sacrifices that are being offered are not able to make the one who worships have a perfected conscience.
These consist only in matters of food, drink, and various ritual washings — these are regulations of the flesh imposed until the time of reform.

But when Christ came as high priest of the good things that have come into being, He went through a tabernacle that is greater and more perfect, which was not made by human hands — that is, not part of this created order —
He did not enter by means of the blood of goats and calves, but He entered by way of His own blood, once for all, into the Most Holy Place; He brought eternal redemption to completion.
For if the blood of goats and bulls, and the ashes of a heifer, which are sprinkled on those who are ritually defiled, make those persons pure in the flesh,

the blood of Christ will accomplish how much more — He who through the eternal Spirit offered Himself unblemished to God — He will purify our consciences from dead works, so that we may serve the living God.

For this reason, Christ is the mediator of a new covenant, so that when a death has occurred to redeem from the transgressions under the first covenant, those who have been called may receive the eternal inheritance that has been promised.
For wherever there is a will-testament, it is necessary that the death of the one who made the will be established.
For a will takes effect only after someone has died; because it cannot have effect as long as the one who made the will is still alive.
For this reason, even the first covenant was not established without blood.

For when Moses had spoken every commandment according to the law to all the people, he took the blood of calves along with water, scarlet wool, and hyssop, and sprinkled both the scroll and all the people,
while saying, 'This is the blood of the covenant which God has commanded to you.'
And in the same way, he sprinkled blood on the tabernacle and on every vessel used in service.
And according to the law, almost everything must be cleansed with blood, and without the shedding of blood, there is no forgiveness of sin.

Therefore it was necessary that the copies of the things in the heavens be cleansed with these sacrifices, but the heavenly things themselves must be cleansed with sacrifices that are better than these.

For Christ did not enter into a Holy Place built by human hands, which is only a representation of the true sanctuary, but He entered into heaven itself, now appearing before the face of God on our behalf.
Furthermore, He did not enter in order to offer Himself many times, like the high priest who enters the Most Holy Place every year using blood that is not his own;

otherwise, He would have had to suffer many times since the foundation of the world. But now, in reality, He has appeared only once, at the end of the ages, in order to abolish sin by means of offering Himself.
And inasmuch as it is appointed that all humans die only once, and after that there is judgment,

in just the same way, Christ was offered only once, in order to bear the sins of many people; and He will come a second time, no longer involved with sin, in order to bring salvation to those who are eagerly waiting for Him.
